text: Hi, I'm Nuan from AWS.
And I'm Ben from Assembly AI.
This is my architecture.
Ben, tell us a little bit about Assembly AI
and type of challenges that you are solving
with this architecture.
Yeah, at Assembly AI, we're laser focused
on developing ML models to understand human speech
with superhuman ability.
In a nutshell, we have a complete AI system
for customers to get the most out of their audio data.
The three challenges that we're trying to solve
with this architecture are scalability,
so handling millions of requests per day,
ease of deployment and iteration,
so developing new models quickly
and updating existing models,
and security and compliance,
making sure we work with customers
to meet their various compliance needs around the globe.
What kind of users interact with the system,
and can you explain the user workflow here?
Yeah, all sorts of users interact with us via an API,
whether it's podcast companies looking to understand
the content on their platforms,
or telephony companies looking to evaluate
and understand call center performance.
It all starts with audio, though.
So customers, Assembly AI users,
upload their binary audio data to our API
or submit a reference to that data
from something like their own S3 bucket.
And then the flow continues from that ECS component,
that's the API manager,
and then moves to the Amazon ECS here.
Can you explain the flow a bit too?
Yeah.
After we've validated the request
and recorded what type of features they want,
Right.
We send that over to our orchestrator.
This thing is the brain of the operation
that really decides what models the call
and in what order.
After we've completed the request for the customer,
we send a notification over SNS,
which is subscribed to by a variety of services,
one of which is this webhook Lambda.
This notifies customers in an asynchronous fashion
that their transcription is completed.
I know there's this part of the architecture
that we didn't talk about,
and I assume the orchestrator here that you talked about
delegates that task into the inferencing here.
Talk to us a little bit about what's happening here.
Yeah. Well, the orchestrator, like I said,
might be the brains of the operation.
All the work gets done over here in this inference pipeline.
We have dozens of models deployed.
We're iterating on them constantly,
so deploying new versions, new models,
really laser-focused on finding ways
to deliver that value back to customers.
So this orchestrator sends messages into SQS,
which drives our actual models running on ECS
to scale up and down and to perform inference on that data.
Okay. How do you use S3 in this architecture?
Yeah. So audio data, as you can imagine, is pretty big.
So we actually pass it by reference from S3 into the ECS container.
After a customer has submitted their data to our API,
we download it, transcode it,
and then it actually gets stored over here in S3.
S3, I see.
So you explained, like, you're using SQS,
and you're building, like, a queue,
and the jobs are getting submitted.
What kind of speed are we talking about?
Yeah. So we really tried to keep the queue empty.
This is an asynchronous system.
So we've created this custom scaling loop here.
It does a couple of things.
It watches the depth of the queue,
as well as predicting when a model is going to get called
and being able to scale it up in time.
So a great example of that would be
a customer requesting speaker labeling.
So who's talking and when,
uh, we know that that comes after converting audio into text.
So we can pre-scale that service,
uh, such that the capacity is there right when we need it.
Tell us a little bit about the ML models you use in this architecture.
Yeah. So most requests start with our flagship,
uh, speech to text model.
Uh, it's called Conformer 2.
Um, this takes that audio binary data
and converts it into text.
From there, uh, we can send it to a variety of,
of different models based on the,
the customer's use case.
Um, so speaker labeling, like I mentioned,
uh, sentiment analysis,
what's the mood of the conversation.
Um, and even into, uh, things like LLMs,
where customers can, uh, ask questions about their audio data.
Yeah. You briefly touched upon the scalability aspect.
Tell us a little bit about when those requests comes in.
How do you scale this, uh, this pipeline over here?
Yeah. So, uh, requests are coming in all the time, uh, millions, millions per day.
And we're, uh, recording that in CloudWatch.
And we know, uh, based on the decision engine inside our orchestrator,
which model and in what order they're going to be called.
Um, so using signals like Q depth, uh, and our custom scaling,
uh, we can provision, uh, more containers, uh, for more models.
So popular models are going to be scaling up and down more quickly than,
than less popular ones, as an example.
In general, these models are pretty expensive running on, on GPU.
So we really like to, to have, uh, good scaling to control cost.
Tell us a little bit about security.
How did you handle security in this architecture?
So there's a lot of, uh, non-functional requirements, uh, compliance, uh, things like that,
that we provide for our customers.
Uh, we're SOC 2 type 2 certified, um, and really care, uh, about following best practices for, for storing data.
Um, we have strict life cycle policies over here in Amazon S3.
Uh, so we, uh, keep the data only as long as, as it's useful, um, for our orchestrator and for our ML pipeline.
That's awesome, Ben.
Thank you so much for, uh, explaining this architecture to us.
I really enjoyed the conversation.
